agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Evolution of the Shipment aggregate from a 1:1 Shipment-Recipient relationship to a 1:N relationship. This is a fundamental change to the core domain model of a delivery platform, affecting the Shipment aggregate root boundary, all downstream bounded contexts that consume shipment data, and external partner integrations. This is not a simple schema migration — it redefines what a Shipment *is*.",
    "bounded_context": "Shipment Management (core domain), with ripple effects into Routing, Notifications, Billing, Tracking, and Partner Integration contexts",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Shipment", "definition": "A unit of goods moving from an origin to one or more delivery destinations. Previously assumed a single destination; now represents a logical grouping of deliveries that share origin, carrier, and lifecycle." },
      { "term": "Recipient", "definition": "A party who receives a delivery. Identified by name, address, phone number, and now also delivery preferences. A Recipient belongs to exactly one Shipment." },
      { "term": "Delivery Preferences", "definition": "Per-recipient instructions governing how delivery is executed — e.g., time windows, signature requirements, safe-place authorization, contactless delivery, notification channel preference." },
      { "term": "Primary Recipient", "definition": "AMBIGUOUS — it is unclear whether multi-recipient shipments designate a primary recipient or treat all recipients equally. This has billing, tracking, and notification implications." },
      { "term": "Shipment Status", "definition": "The lifecycle state of a shipment. Previously singular (one delivery = one status). Must now reconcile with per-recipient delivery outcomes that may differ." },
      { "term": "Partner Integration", "definition": "External API contracts with partners who consume shipment data in the current single-recipient format." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "A Shipment must have at least one Recipient at all times. A Shipment with zero Recipients is invalid.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A shipment without a recipient has no delivery destination — it cannot exist as a domain concept. This is a structural integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Any attempt to create a Shipment with an empty Recipients collection, or to remove the last Recipient from a Shipment, must be rejected."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Each Recipient within a Shipment must have a valid name, address, and phone number.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "These are the minimum identifiers required to execute a physical delivery. Without them, the courier cannot complete the delivery and the system state is corrupt.",
      "testable_assertion": "Creating or updating a Recipient with a null/empty name, address, or phone number must be rejected at the aggregate boundary."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Delivery preferences are optional per Recipient. If not specified, system defaults apply.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Default preferences are a business policy decision — what constitutes a 'default' can change (e.g., contactless delivery became default during COVID). A VP can update defaults.",
      "testable_assertion": "A Recipient created without explicit delivery preferences must have system default preferences applied. The Shipment must still be valid."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Existing single-recipient shipments must remain valid and fully functional after the model change. No data loss, no behavioral regression.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "2 million production shipments represent real business commitments. Corrupting or invalidating them violates data integrity and real-world delivery obligations.",
      "testable_assertion": "All 2 million existing shipments must be queryable, updatable, and deliverable after migration. Each must present its existing recipient as a single-element collection with no data loss on name, address, or phone."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "External partner APIs must continue to receive shipment data they can process. Breaking partner contracts is a business-critical failure.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Partner API contracts are legal/commercial obligations. A breaking change could trigger SLA violations, revenue loss, and partner churn. This is a system integrity concern beyond just technical compatibility.",
      "testable_assertion": "Partners consuming the current single-recipient format must continue to receive valid payloads. For multi-recipient shipments, the integration layer must define a clear serialization strategy (e.g., one webhook per recipient, or a designated primary recipient)."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Shipment-level status must be derivable from the aggregate state of all recipient delivery outcomes.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The specific derivation rules (e.g., 'complete when all recipients delivered' vs 'complete when any recipient delivered') are business policy. But some derivation rule must exist.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a shipment with 3 recipients where 2 are delivered and 1 failed, the system must compute a shipment-level status according to the defined policy — not leave it undefined."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "Each Recipient within a Shipment must have an independently trackable delivery status.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If recipients can have different addresses and preferences, their delivery outcomes will differ. A single status for all recipients would make the system unable to represent physical reality — a recipient whose package was delivered cannot show as 'pending' because another recipient's delivery failed.",
      "testable_assertion": "Each Recipient in a multi-recipient Shipment must have its own status field. Status transitions for one Recipient must not automatically change another Recipient's status."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A new shipment is being created",
      "when": "The caller provides two or more recipients, each with name, address, phone, and optional delivery preferences",
      "then": "The shipment is created successfully with all recipients persisted, each with their own delivery preferences and an initial delivery status"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A new shipment is being created",
      "when": "The caller provides zero recipients",
      "then": "The creation is rejected with a validation error indicating at least one recipient is required"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "An existing single-recipient shipment created before the model change",
      "when": "The shipment is queried via the API",
      "then": "The shipment is returned with its recipient in a collection of one, with all original data (name, address, phone) intact and default delivery preferences applied"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A multi-recipient shipment exists",
      "when": "A delivery is completed for one recipient but not others",
      "then": "The delivered recipient's status is updated independently; other recipients retain their current status; the shipment-level status reflects the partial completion per business policy"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "An external partner is subscribed to shipment webhooks",
      "when": "A multi-recipient shipment is created or updated",
      "then": "The partner receives data in a format compatible with their current integration (backward-compatible payload or versioned endpoint), with no data loss and no deserialization errors on the partner side"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A multi-recipient shipment with 3 recipients",
      "when": "The caller attempts to remove all 3 recipients",
      "then": "The operation is rejected, preserving the invariant that a shipment must have at least one recipient"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "A recipient with specific delivery preferences (e.g., 'leave at door, no signature')",
      "when": "The courier completes delivery for that recipient",
      "then": "The delivery workflow honors that recipient's specific preferences, not the preferences of other recipients on the same shipment"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Multi-recipient shipment where all recipients share the same address", "expected_behavior": "Valid — this represents multiple packages or items going to different people at the same location (e.g., office building). Each recipient still has independent status and preferences." },
    { "scenario": "Recipient added to a shipment that is already in-transit", "expected_behavior": "Must be governed by business policy — likely rejected or only allowed before carrier pickup. Adding a recipient mid-transit changes routing and has physical-world constraints." },
    { "scenario": "Multi-recipient shipment where one recipient's address is undeliverable", "expected_behavior": "That recipient is marked as failed/returned. Other recipients' deliveries proceed independently. Shipment-level status reflects partial failure." },
    { "scenario": "Partner API receives a multi-recipient shipment but only supports single-recipient format", "expected_behavior": "The integration adapter must apply a defined strategy: fan-out into N single-recipient payloads, or send only the primary recipient, or reject if no strategy exists. Silent data loss is unacceptable." },
    { "scenario": "Billing for a multi-recipient shipment", "expected_behavior": "Must be defined: is it billed as one shipment, or per-recipient? If per-recipient, what is the pricing model? This likely requires Billing context changes." },
    { "scenario": "Duplicate recipients on the same shipment (same name, address, phone)", "expected_behavior": "Should be allowed — represents legitimate business cases (e.g., two separate orders for the same person). But should warn or require confirmation to prevent data entry errors." },
    { "scenario": "Shipment tracking page for end customers", "expected_behavior": "A recipient should only see their own delivery status, not other recipients' details. Privacy boundary must be enforced — one recipient must not see another's name, address, or phone." },
    { "scenario": "Migration of 2M existing shipments during deployment", "expected_behavior": "Must be a non-destructive, backward-compatible migration. Preferably additive (new column/table) rather than transformative. Zero-downtime migration required for a production system of this scale." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "High impact. Multi-recipient shipments enable new revenue models (bulk/group shipping, multi-drop delivery pricing). However, billing model ambiguity is a risk — if not defined before launch, revenue leakage or overcharging is possible. Migration costs are non-trivial: engineering effort, testing across 2M records, partner re-integration.",
    "operational": "Significant. Routing and dispatch must now optimize for multi-stop deliveries per shipment. Courier apps must display per-recipient instructions. Customer support must handle partial delivery states ('2 of 3 delivered'). Reporting and analytics must account for the new cardinality.",
    "user": "Positive for senders who ship to multiple recipients in one transaction. Potentially confusing for recipients who now share a tracking number — or beneficial if each gets their own tracking view. Courier experience changes significantly with per-recipient preferences.",
    "risk": "HIGH. This is a foundational model change affecting the core aggregate. Key risks: (1) Data migration failure corrupting 2M shipments, (2) Partner API breaking changes causing SLA violations, (3) Undefined shipment-level status logic creating inconsistent states, (4) Privacy violations if recipients can see each other's data, (5) Billing model undefined leading to revenue loss. Recommend phased rollout with feature flag.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Senders/Shippers", "impact": "New capability — can create multi-recipient shipments. Must understand new pricing and tracking model." },
      { "group": "Recipients", "impact": "May receive deliveries as part of a group shipment. Privacy must be protected. Per-recipient tracking must be clear." },
      { "group": "Couriers/Drivers", "impact": "Must handle per-recipient delivery preferences within a single shipment. Route optimization changes. App UX must surface per-stop instructions." },
      { "group": "External Partners", "impact": "API contract change. Must be communicated with lead time. Breaking changes could violate SLAs and damage commercial relationships." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Must handle new inquiry types: partial deliveries, per-recipient issues, multi-recipient disputes. Training and tooling required." },
      { "group": "Finance/Billing", "impact": "Pricing model for multi-recipient shipments must be defined. Revenue recognition may change. Invoicing format affected." },
      { "group": "Product/Engineering", "impact": "Significant effort across multiple bounded contexts. Must coordinate migration, API versioning, and feature rollout." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 45,
    "reasoning": "The spec is dangerously incomplete. It states the structural change (1:N recipients with delivery preferences) but leaves critical business semantics undefined: What constitutes shipment-level status? How are partners handled? What is the billing model? Is there a primary recipient concept? What are the privacy rules? These are not implementation details — they are domain decisions that fundamentally shape correctness. Without them, engineers will guess, and different engineers will guess differently.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "At-least-one-recipient invariant is correct and non-negotiable",
      "Backward compatibility with 2M existing shipments is mandatory",
      "Per-recipient delivery status tracking is necessary consequence of the model change",
      "Partner API backward compatibility is a hard requirement"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Shipment-level status derivation rules — not specified",
      "Billing model for multi-recipient shipments — not specified",
      "Primary recipient concept — not defined, but many downstream systems may need it",
      "Privacy boundaries between recipients on the same shipment — not addressed",
      "Maximum number of recipients per shipment — no upper bound defined",
      "Whether recipients can be added/removed after shipment creation — lifecycle rules missing"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Delivery preferences include at minimum: time window, signature requirement, and safe-place authorization — inferred from industry standard, not from spec",
      "Existing single-recipient shipments will be migrated in-place rather than dual-written — assumed from 'existing data in production' framing",
      "Partners cannot be broken without notice — assumed from standard B2B API practices",
      "Each recipient maps to a distinct physical delivery stop — assumed, but could be wrong if recipients share addresses"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Shipment-level status derivation is undefined. When a shipment has 3 recipients and deliveries are in different states (1 delivered, 1 failed, 1 pending), what is the shipment status? Options include: 'partial', 'in-progress until all complete', 'complete when any completes', or a composite status. This affects every system that reads shipment status — tracking pages, partner webhooks, reporting, SLA calculations.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Senders", "Recipients", "Customer Support", "Partners", "Reporting/Analytics"],
      "options": ["Introduce a 'partially_delivered' shipment status", "Keep shipment status as latest-event-driven and add per-recipient status as the source of truth", "Derive shipment status as 'completed' only when ALL recipients are delivered"],
      "recommendation": "Introduce explicit per-recipient status as the primary status, with shipment-level status derived as 'completed only when all recipients completed or resolved'. This matches physical reality and is the least surprising to all stakeholders."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The concept of 'Primary Recipient' is not addressed. Many downstream processes assume a single point of contact for a shipment: billing address, tracking notifications, return-to-sender flows, dispute resolution. Without defining whether multi-recipient shipments have a primary recipient, these processes have no clear target.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Billing/Finance", "Customer Support", "Partners"],
      "options": ["Designate first recipient as primary by default", "Require explicit primary designation on creation", "Eliminate the concept and make all processes per-recipient"],
      "recommendation": "Require explicit primary recipient designation. This preserves backward compatibility (existing single-recipient is implicitly primary) and gives downstream systems a clear fallback contact."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Billing model for multi-recipient shipments is entirely unspecified. Is it one charge for the shipment regardless of recipients? Per-recipient pricing? Tiered pricing? This must be a product/business decision — engineering cannot and should not guess.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Finance/Billing", "Senders", "Product"],
      "options": ["Flat rate per shipment regardless of recipient count", "Per-recipient pricing with volume discounts", "Base shipment fee plus per-additional-recipient surcharge"],
      "recommendation": "Escalate to Product and Finance before implementation. Recommend base-plus-surcharge model as it aligns with industry norms and incentivizes the feature without underpricing multi-drop logistics cost."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Partner API migration strategy is unspecified. Partners currently receive single-recipient payloads. Options range from API versioning (v1 stays single-recipient, v2 supports multi) to fan-out (one webhook per recipient) to requiring partner migration. Each has different cost, timeline, and risk profiles.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["External Partners", "Engineering", "Business Development"],
      "options": ["API versioning with v1 backward compatibility layer", "Fan-out: emit one partner event per recipient using existing format", "Require partners to migrate to new format with deprecation timeline"],
      "recommendation": "API versioning with a backward-compatible v1 adapter that fans out multi-recipient shipments as N single-recipient events. This is zero-disruption for partners while the new v2 format is adopted organically."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "No upper bound on recipients per shipment is defined. Without a limit, a single shipment could have thousands of recipients, creating performance, routing, and UX problems. But setting the limit too low restricts legitimate business use cases.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Couriers", "Senders"],
      "options": ["No limit (dangerous)", "Hard limit of 10 recipients (covers most multi-drop scenarios)", "Configurable per-customer limit based on their plan/tier"],
      "recommendation": "Set a default limit of 20 recipients per shipment, configurable per customer. This covers legitimate multi-drop scenarios while preventing abuse. The limit is a business policy (invariant: false) and can be adjusted."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
